content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

updated_tasks.search_intent: Vergleich Wayside vs Onboard für typische Messfälle (Hotbox, Wheel Impact, Profil, Lager, Bremsen): Leistungskennwerte und Grenzen sammeln.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Ermöglicht Mapping der Messfunktionen und Performance-Anforderungen (Genauigkeit, Erfassungsrate, Fehlalarme) für Transferentscheidungen.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Technische Randbedingungen für Rolling-Stock-in-Motion-Sensorik: Vibration, Temperatur, EMV, Schutzart, Montage am Drehgestell/Radsatz.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Identifiziert Umwelt- und Integrationsbedingungen, die Wayside-Technologien beim Onboard-Einsatz limitieren oder Anpassungen erzwingen.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Energieversorgung, Datenlogger/Edge-AI und Kommunikation (TCMS, Ethernet, GSM-R/FRMCS) für Onboard-Monitoring im Betrieb recherchieren.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Klären der Systemarchitektur-Voraussetzungen (Strom, Rechenleistung, Schnittstellen, Datenübertragung) für skalierbaren Transfer.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: , airlines, OEMs, MROs, and other stakeholders have to compare the cost of adoption (Barrier 3) against the benefits such a program could provide. This Section deals with the estimation of the latter. 6.1 Challenges Estimating the impact of predictive maintenance in commercial aviation is a complex endeavor. Challenges that stakeholders identified include: 1. Life Cycle Cost Modeling There is a lack of accurate and reliable life cycle cost and risk modeling (Ref. 11, 32). [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022][Brian Tucker, Bell, 2022][Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Wes Ryan, Former FAA, 2023] Life cycle costs include acquisition, operation, maintenance, and expected future repair and replacement. Life cycle cost modeling help airlines understand the impact of PdM and assess if its cost are worth the benefits of reduced downtime and reduction of unforeseen repairs. 63% of companies not working on implementing PdM indicate that their reason for not doing so was because of their inability to quantify the impact/business case (Ref. 9). Without this data, it is difficult for OEMs to tailor aircraft component design (including sensor integration) and manufacturing to reduce maintenance costs. Risk modeling examines how maintenance techniques may affect aircraft safety and dependability. Without proper comprehension of the potential risks and the likelihood of their occurrence, airlines are hindered in making informed decisions regarding optimal maintenance strategies. To this date, there exists neither a standardized methodology for the simulation of an aircraft’s life cycle, nor a widely used standardized methodology for the estimation of their life cycle cost [Aircraft OEM, 2022]. Some best practices, although rather general in nature, can be found in SAE ARP 4293 and SAE AIR 1939. 2. Uncertainty Modeling Uncertainty in the life cycle simulation (including, but not limited to, future price developments, expected demands, and the environment) leads to inaccurate predictions and reduced trust in the overall benefit of predictive maintenance [Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022]. These uncertainties can be of aleatory nature (i.e., not reducible, as it stems from the system’s natural randomness) or of epistemic nature (i.e., potentially reducible, as it stems from a lack of knowledge). Difficulties include the

domain_applicability.reason: Allgemeines Problem der Life-Cycle-Cost- und Risikomodellierung als Barriere für Predictive Maintenance; domänenübergreifend auf Rail übertragbar.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. RFID information flow. Table 1. Rail-TAG transponder specifications. all_chunks.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

domain_applicability.reason: Abkürzungsverzeichnis mit stark aviation-spezifischen Begriffen/Institutionen (FAA, MEL, AHM); wenig fachlicher Mehrwert für Rail.

all_chunks.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. Thus, the safe train can be calculated by combining the HPL and error range of two the envelope confidence interval as (5) 5. State perception performance evaluation 5.1. RAMS architecture for railway signaling The performance indices of GNSS-based positioning are different from the requirements of railway systems, especially for the safety-related railway applications. With the EN50126 standard definition, the RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability & Safety) architecture is presented with different requirements to specific railway systems and functions. 1) Reliability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform a specified function under specified conditions and within a specified time period. The evaluation indicators with respect to the reliability include failure rate, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failure) and MUT (Mean Up Time). 2) Availability. It refers to the extent to which a product or a system is in a workable or usable state at any moment when the required external re- sources are met or when it starts to perform its tasks. The availability of a product is a composite representation of its reliability and maintainability. 3) Maintainability. It refers to the ability of a product or a system to perform specified maintenance work under the specified conditions and within the specified time using the specified procedures and resources for maintenance. 4) Safety. It refers to the ability to not incur risks that may cause damage. The safety requirements of the GNSS autonomous positioning- based train control system include functional safety requirements and other safety requirements. Among them, functional safety requirements are described by SIL (Safety Integrity Level), and different SIL levels are defined by the THR (Tolerable Hazard Rate). For a novel train control system based on autonomous perception using GNSS, the RAMS indices also have to be considered in the design and operation phases. The relationship of RAMS indices in the railway domain is shown in Fig. 7. If the reliability and maintainability are met, and long-term maintenance and control are performed according to the environment in which the system is located, the safety and availability of the system can also be met. 51 =zxgkkf[(),()](

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: 4. Train integrity monitoring The connection mode between the train carriages is a rigid connection. When the train is grouped, the length of the train will be fixed covering the length of all the locomotives and carriages (or EMUs for the high-speed railway) [10,11]. In the practical operation, a normal train integrity state indicates that the relative speed of the HOT (Head-of-Train) and EOT (End-of-Train) is zero and the projected HOT-EOT interval equals to the nominal train length. When decoupling occurs, the train will be in an incomplete state, the thus the EOT will lose the traction force and de- celerate until the speed falls to zero. An abnormal integrity status will result in an increased HOT-EOT relative speed, leading to an increasing HOT-EOT distance. Therefore, the train integrity state can be identified by evaluating the HOT/EOT relevant state and the train length. 4.1. Train integrity monitoring based on GNSS and trackmap Through the monitoring of along-track position, speed and direction of the HOT and EOT with the aid of the trackmap database, the train integrity status can be determined through the dynamic evaluation of the train length. Safety stands for the ability to eliminate the risk of un- acceptable damage to a positioning unit. The railway sector uses safety integrity level to describe the likelihood that a system achieves the required safety function within a specified time under all specified conditions. The SIL specifies the safety integrity requirement for the safety functions assigned to the safety-related systems. Different SIL levels can be described in Table 5. The risk assessment needs to be performed for the safe design of train positioning architecture for the railway signaling applications. The risk assessment results can be classified as risky and the risk-free cases. Therefore, the output of safety assessment can be defined as the frequency of risky event Prisk. It has to be noted that the above-mentioned indices considering both the RAMS and GNSS systems are closely correlated, which means that performance evaluation is carried out based on a systemic measure. The relationship among the performance indicators for GNSS-based train positioning and state perception can be described as Fig. 8. 6. Field demonstration and analysis Based on the development of the novel train control system, an advanced train positioning unit for the railway train control applica- tion was designed and developed. The unit is realized based on the safe platform with a 2-out-of-2 (2oo2) redundant architecture. Under the safe platform architecture, the sensor data collection boards and communication boards are designed independently. Two sensor data collection boards are involved to independently collect raw measure- ments from the GPS/BDS receivers, INS sensors and the odometer. The communication boards are designed for information interaction be- tween the train positioning unit and the ATP kernel. The core position calculation unit realizes the sensor data fusion and location determi- nation using the presented technologies in this paper. In 2021, a field test was carried out at the Haergai-Muli railway. The test section is more than 120 kilometers with a high altitude between 3200 and 4200 m. Five stations are involved in the field test section, including Huancang, Chaidaer II, Chaidaer I, Wailihada and Jiangcang. Trackmap database of the test section was measured and generated before the field demonstration. Specific track-side GPS/BDS differ- ential stations were developed and installed to enhance the perfor- mance of train-borne receivers. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: data volume, which releases the require- ment to the train-ground communication and the complexity of system interfaces for trackmap verification. Through the integration of static trackmap information, sensor data fusion for BDS/GPS re- ceivers, INS sensors and the odometer measurements were real- timely achieved with a high availability level under the complex and time-changing operation environment. Both the static and dynamic test cases were involved in the field demonstration. Table 6 sum- marizes the accuracy results of train positioning under different system modes. The results from field demonstration illustrate that involvement of the presented key technologies, including the safe platform and multi- source sensor fusion mechanism, effectively guarantees the perfor- mance of GNSS-based autonomous train positioning and state percep- tion even under complex and time-varying operational conditions. The utilization of the differential GNSS technology and the integrity mon- itoring logic makes it possible of achieving the high accuracy and ro- bustness against the possible difficult or even critical conditions. The field test results demonstrate the feasibility and the advantages of utilizing BDS in realizing a novel autonomous train control system with a train-centric design, which is of great significance in the future high- speed and plateau railway applications with the enhanced environ- mental flexibility and whole life cycle cost efficiency. 7. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. Therefore, the optimization index of train-to-train communication radio resource management should be based on the QoS requirements of CBTC services, that is, higher require- ments for reliability but lower requirements for data rate. After the introduction of train-to-train communication, the urban rail transit wireless communication system has become more complicated, which puts forward higher requirements for radio resource management as well. How to improve the service quality of the train-to-train com- munication train carrying service based on service priority is the optimization goal of radio resource management in this paper. D2D communication is divided into centralized control and distributed control. Compared with distributed control, centralized control not only can take advantage of D2D communication, but can also facilitate the management and control of resources. Therefore, in this scenario, the train- to-train communication mode adopts centralized control. When train-to-train communication and train-to-wayside communication coexist and the same time-frequency resources are multiplexed, the two communication links will interfere with each other. In the resource multiplexing mode, it can be divided into two situations: multiplexing train-to-wayside communication uplink resources and downlink resources [19]. In Fig. 3, the D2D link (Sidelink) between train 2 and train 3 multiplex the uplink Uu channel resources of train 1. Train 2 is used as the transmitter of the D2D link, and train 3 is used as the receiver. The interference situa- tion in the system is divided into two parts: the Uu link of the base station is interfered by the transmission power of the Sidelink link of train 2, and the Sidelink is interfered by the running channel on the Uu link of the train-to-wayside communication of train 1. The next step is to study the multiplexing of D2D Sidelink link and establish a resource scheduling model with Fig. 3 as an application scenario. 3.1.2 Optimization of Problem Transformation Different from the public network that pursues fairness in resource allocation, the number of urban rail transit users is small and the priorities of different train services are dif- ferent. Train-to-train communication is used to bear the CBTC service with the highest priority, while train-to- wayside communication is used to bear PIS and CCTV services with lower reliability requirements. Therefore, in the Stackelberg model game, the train-to-train communi- cation link is the leader so as to encourage the establish- ment of the D2D link to improve the reliability of the CBTC business. Since the D2D link and the train-to-way- side communication link multiplex wireless resources, the train-to-wayside communication will cause interference to the train-to-train communication link, which may affect the transmission rate of the CBTC service. Therefore, the train- to-wayside communication is deﬁned as a follower. On the basis of ensuring that the CBTC service is not affected, the throughput of video services such as PIS is increased as much as possible to maximize the link revenue. The rest of this paper is summarized as follows. The feasibility of implementing train-to-train communication is analyzed in Sect. 2. Section 3 proposes a radio resource management algorithm based on the Stackelberg game model, which effectively improves the reliability of train CBTC service under the coexistence of train-to-wayside and train-to-train communication modes. Simulation results and discussions are given in Sect. 4. Finally, we conclude the study in Sect. 5. 2 Feasibility Analysis of Train-to-Train Communication In this section the feasibility of the train-to-train commu- nication mode is analyzed from two aspects: the wireless signal coverage of the train as the transmitter and the train interval in the actual operation scene of the urban rail transit train. 2.1 Wireless Signal Coverage In the CBTC system, the train antenna is located at the front or rear of the train, it interacts with ground equipment to realize two-way communication between the train and the ground. The LTE-M system equipment technical speciﬁcation [16] points out that the train shall meet the requirements for train-to-wayside communication when the received signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) value is greater than or equal to -2 dB. preview.query:
Welche Voraussetzungen/Faktoren müssen für einen Transfer von Technologie Wayside-Sensorik (Streckenseitige Sensoren) in Bereich Rolling
Stock in Motion erfüllt sein ?
preview.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf Voraussetzungen für den Technologietransfer von streckenseitiger Zustands-/Objekterfassung (Wayside) hin zu fahrzeugseitiger Sensorik am rollenden Rad/zugseitig…

input.query:
Welche Voraussetzungen/Faktoren müssen für einen Transfer von Technologie Wayside-Sensorik (Streckenseitige Sensoren) in Bereich Rolling
Stock in Motion erfüllt sein ?
input.research_plan_reasoning: Die Frage zielt auf Voraussetzungen für den Technologietransfer von streckenseitiger Zustands-/Objekterfassung (Wayside) hin zu fahrzeugseitiger Sensorik am rollenden Rad/zugseitig (Rolling Stock in Motion). Relevante Faktoren sind funktionale Äquivalenz (Messgrößen/Genauigkeit), Randbedingungen (Vibration, EMV, Energie, Montage), Datenverarbeitung/Kommunikation, Safety/Assurance (SIL, RAMS) sowie Zulassung/Standards und Betrieb/ Instandhaltung über den Lebenszyklus.

research_aspects.description: Transfer auf fahrzeugseitige Sensorik erfordert Nachweis von funktionaler Sicherheit, RAMS-Compliance und Erfüllung einschlägiger Bahnstandards sowie Zulassungsprozesse (Onboard-Änderung am Fahrzeug).

Zu klären ist, ob Onboard-Sensorik die Wayside-Funktionen (z.B. Hotbox, Wheel Impact, Profil, Lager/Bremse) mit vergleichbarer Sensitivität, Genauigkeit, Fehlalarmrate und Erkennungsgrenzen leisten kann.

research_aspects.aspect: Umgebungs- und Integrationsrandbedingungen am Rolling Stock
research_aspects.description: Onboard-Implementierung muss mechanische/thermische Belastungen (Vibration, Schock, Temperatur), EMV, Schutzart sowie Montage- und Bauraumrestriktionen an Drehgestell/Radsatz beherrschen.
Voraussetzungen sind robuste Signalverarbeitung/Edge-AI, Schnittstellen ins TCMS, zuverlässige Übertragung (z.B. Ethernet, GSM-R/FRMCS) sowie Datenintegrität und IT/OT-Sicherheitsanforderungen.
Erfolgreicher Transfer hängt von Kalibrierbarkeit, Datenqualität, Wartungsaufwand, Ersatzteil-/Update-Strategien, Prozessintegration in CBM/RCM und Lebenszykluskosten gegenüber Wayside ab.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. RFID information flow. Table 1. Rail-TAG transponder specifications. Type RAIL-TAG Size Weight Mounting Location Frequency Energy supply Lifetime Power consumption Reading distance Certificates Reading accuracy Applicable speeds Temperature 130x28x20 mm (LxWxH) 50 gr 5 mm screw of gluing According TSI, right+left 868 MHz (UHF) Internal > 6 years < 1 mW Up to 50 m CE (EN300220-1); ETSI EN3001489-1 Better than 99.9% Up to 230 km/h -30°C to +70°C Table 2. Rail-READER specifications. Type RRDR-Gen2 Size Enclosure Weight Mounting Position Water resistance Temperature Energy supply Power consumption Sensors Serviced tracks Communication Protocols available Protocol ready Interface Monitoring 280x240

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Basically the force sensor represents a 1140mm long P49E1 rail section with milled surfaces (sensitive areas) on which a total of four installed. As the output signal of the tensometric bridges is of very small amplitude, a differential instrumental amplifier was included [2]. (standby) The sensor features two power modes – Low Power Mode and Normal Mode (measuring). The switching between the two modes is achieved by using rail contacts, installed on the track before and after the check-point measuring zone. The rail contacts also serve as axle counters. The electronics of the specialized force sensor is contained in a metal box, mounted on the rail section (Fig. 6). Specifications of the specialized force sensor are listed in Table 3. Table 3. content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: , airlines, OEMs, MROs, and other stakeholders have to compare the cost of adoption (Barrier 3) against the benefits such a program could provide. This Section deals with the estimation of the latter. 6.1 Challenges Estimating the impact of predictive maintenance in commercial aviation is a complex endeavor. Challenges that stakeholders identified include: 1. Life Cycle Cost Modeling There is a lack of accurate and reliable life cycle cost and risk modeling (Ref. 11, 32). [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022][Brian Tucker, Bell, 2022][Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Wes Ryan, Former FAA, 2023] Life cycle costs include acquisition, operation, maintenance, and expected future repair and replacement. Life cycle cost modeling help airlines understand the impact of PdM and assess if its cost are worth the benefits of reduced downtime and reduction of unforeseen repairs. 63% of companies not working on implementing PdM indicate that their reason for not doing so was because of their inability to quantify the impact/business case (Ref. 9). Without this data, it is difficult for OEMs to tailor aircraft component design (including sensor integration) and manufacturing to reduce maintenance costs. Risk modeling examines how maintenance techniques may affect aircraft safety and dependability. Without proper comprehension of the potential risks and the likelihood of their occurrence, airlines are hindered in making informed decisions regarding optimal maintenance strategies. To this date, there exists neither a standardized methodology for the simulation of an aircraft’s life cycle, nor a widely used standardized methodology for the estimation of their life cycle cost [Aircraft OEM, 2022]. Some best practices, although rather general in nature, can be found in SAE ARP 4293 and SAE AIR 1939. 2. Uncertainty Modeling Uncertainty in the life cycle simulation (including, but not limited to, future price developments, expected demands, and the environment) leads to inaccurate predictions and reduced trust in the overall benefit of predictive maintenance [Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022]. These uncertainties can be of aleatory nature (i.e., not reducible, as it stems from the system’s natural randomness) or of epistemic nature (i.e., potentially reducible, as it stems from a lack of knowledge). Difficulties include the

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

metadata.task_query: Betriebs- und Instandhaltungsfaktoren: Kalibrierung, Datenqualität, Fehlalarme, Lebenszykluskosten und Prozessintegration (CBM/RCM) untersuchen. CBM RCM alarm management sensor calibration life cycle cost
metadata.task_rationale: Ergänzt technische Anforderungen um organisatorische/ökonomische Faktoren, die Transfer in den Regelbetrieb ermöglichen.

domain_applicability.reason: Nur Luftfahrt-Abkürzungen; keine allgemeinen Betriebs-/Instandhaltungsfaktoren beschrieben.

hypotheses.statement: Ein Transfer von Wayside- zu Rolling-Stock-in-Motion-Sensorik ist wirtschaftlich/organisatorisch nur dann skalierbar, wenn für das neue Onboard-Sensorsystem belastbare Lebenszykluskosten- und Risikomodelle (Acquisition/Operation/Maintenance/Replacement sowie Safety/Dependability-Risiken) vorliegen, da fehlende standardisierte LCC-/Risikomodellierung die Quantifizierung des Business Case und damit die Adoptionsentscheidung verhindert.
hypotheses.reasoning: NASA-TM20230000841 beschreibt als Adoptionsbarriere für Predictive Maintenance (inkl. Sensorintegration) den Mangel an akkurater/verlässlicher Life-Cycle-Cost- und Risikomodellierung; 63% der nicht implementierenden Unternehmen nennen fehlende Fähigkeit zur Business-Case-Quantifizierung als Grund. Übertragbar als Voraussetzung für Onboard-Sensorik-Umstieg: Nachweis von Nutzen vs. Adoptionskosten über den Lebenszyklus inkl. Risikobetrachtung.

hypotheses.statement: Für funktionale Äquivalenz bei der Zustandsüberwachung (z.B. Hotbox/Wheels/Brakes) müssen Onboard-Lösungen eine Messleistung erreichen, die mindestens den im Wayside-Kontext genutzten Sensorparametern entspricht (z.B. Infrarotmessung mit integrierter Autokalibrierung, definierter Messfrequenz/Speed-Bereich sowie Umwelt-Temperaturbereich), sonst sinkt Detektionsqualität bei hohen Geschwindigkeiten und wechselnden Umweltbedingungen.
hypotheses.reasoning: ISSE 2009 spezifiziert für Wayside-Hotbox/Wheel/Brake-Detektion kommerzielle IR-Sensoren mit u.a. integrierter Autokalibrierung, Messfrequenz (250 Hz) und Einsatz bis hohe Zuggeschwindigkeiten sowie Ambient-Temperaturbereich. Diese Parameter bilden eine prüfbare Untergrenze/Benchmark für Onboard-Funktionalität, wenn Wayside-Funktionalität ersetzt werden soll.

hypotheses.statement: Eine zentrale Voraussetzung für den Transfer auf Rolling Stock in Motion ist eine datenreduzierende Edge-Verarbeitung mit standardisiertem, kompaktem Ergebnisformat (z.B. XML) und priorisierter Alarmübermittlung, da bereits Wayside-Systeme zur Minimierung der Übertragungsdatenmenge lokale Verarbeitung nutzen und Alarme mit Priorität an Betriebsstellen/Signalisierung senden.
hypotheses.reasoning: ISSE 2009 beschreibt, dass Wayside-Systeme Rohdaten lokal verarbeiten (wenige Minuten), Ergebnisse/Alarme per LAN/Internet (u.a. GSM-R) senden und zur Datenmengenreduktion XML-Protokoll verwenden; kritische Alarme werden priorisiert an nächste Station und Signalisierung gesendet. Für Onboard-Transfer folgt als prüfbare Voraussetzung: Edge-Verarbeitung und priorisierte, schlanke Kommunikation statt Rohdaten-Streaming.

hypotheses.statement: Die Übernahme streckenseitiger Identifikationsfunktionen in Richtung Rolling Stock erfordert robuste Identifikations-/Zuordnungsmechanismen (z.B. RFID), weil optische Verfahren (OCR) in realen Betriebsbedingungen (Verschmutzung/Zustand der Beschilderung, Geschwindigkeit, Wetter/Licht) unzuverlässig sind; ein Transfer muss daher eine technisch belastbare Fahrzeug-/Achszuordnung sicherstellen.
hypotheses.reasoning: ISSE 2009 berichtet über erfolglose OCR-Trials aufgrund verschmutzter/korrodierter Kennzeichnungen, hoher Geschwindigkeit und Wetter-/Lichteinflüsse; als Alternative wird RFID (Rail-TAG/Rail-READER) gewählt und mit >99,9% Reading Accuracy bis 230 km/h spezifiziert. Für den Technologie-Transfer ist damit die robuste Identifikation/Zuordnung als Voraussetzung evidenzbasiert.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. RFID information flow. Table 1. Rail-TAG transponder specifications. content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. content.title: WIK Diskussionsbeitrag Nr 315 01
content.abstract_or_snippet: I-Schicht 2) z.B. ATM-, Ethernet-, oder WDM-basiert sein kann. QoS kann komplementär zu den drei Strate- gien auch auf tieferen OSI-Schichten unterstützt werden. In dieser Studie werden daher QoS bei Ethernet und Multi Protocol Label Switching (MPLS) sowie in kürzerer Form ATM berücksichtigt. Abbildung 2-2 ordnet alle Verfahren in das OSI-Schichtenmodell ein. Abbildung 2-2: Systematisierung der QoS-Strategien anhand des ISO/OSI bzw. TCP/IP Schichtenmodells OSI Schicht TCP/IP Schicht Protokoll Instrumente/ Strategien Application (Anwendungsschicht) Presentation (Darstellungsschicht) Session (Sitzungs- bzw. Kommunikationsschicht) t r e i t n e i r o s g n u d n e w n A Transport (Transportschicht) Network (Netzwerk- bzw. Vermittlungsschicht) Data Link (Sicherungsschicht) t r e i t n e i r o t r o p s n a r T Anwendung Transport Internet Physical (Bitübertragungsschicht) Netzzugang 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 Quelle: WIK HTTP FTP HTTPS NCP TCP UDP SPX RSVP ICMP IGMP IP IPX Ethernet ATM Frame Relay SDH WDM IntServ über RSVP DiffServ Kennzeichnung im IP-Header IEEE 802.1q Implementierung im Ethernet-Frame MPLS Strategien zur Realisierung von Quality of Service in IP-Netzen 9 Im Anschluss werden die drei wesentlichen Realisierungsstrategien für QoS in IP Net- zen beschrieben (Kapitel 2.1.1 - 2.1.2) und danach hinsichtlich ihrer Stärken und Schwächen verglichen (Kapitel 2.2). Anschließend werden die komplementären Ansät- ze zu QoS mit Ethernet und MPLS in Kapitel 2.3 sowie die bisher zur QoS Implementie- rung vorherrschende ATM-Technik beschrieben. Zu beachten ist, dass die hier thematisierten Verfahren zwar einen wesentlichen Teil der Einflussfaktoren auf

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: , airlines, OEMs, MROs, and other stakeholders have to compare the cost of adoption (Barrier 3) against the benefits such a program could provide. This Section deals with the estimation of the latter. 6.1 Challenges Estimating the impact of predictive maintenance in commercial aviation is a complex endeavor. Challenges that stakeholders identified include: 1. Life Cycle Cost Modeling There is a lack of accurate and reliable life cycle cost and risk modeling (Ref. 11, 32). [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022][Brian Tucker, Bell, 2022][Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Wes Ryan, Former FAA, 2023] Life cycle costs include acquisition, operation, maintenance, and expected future repair and replacement. Life cycle cost modeling help airlines understand the impact of PdM and assess if its cost are worth the benefits of reduced downtime and reduction of unforeseen repairs. 63% of companies not working on implementing PdM indicate that their reason for not doing so was because of their inability to quantify the impact/business case (Ref. 9). Without this data, it is difficult for OEMs to tailor aircraft component design (including sensor integration) and manufacturing to reduce maintenance costs. Risk modeling examines how maintenance techniques may affect aircraft safety and dependability. Without proper comprehension of the potential risks and the likelihood of their occurrence, airlines are hindered in making informed decisions regarding optimal maintenance strategies. To this date, there exists neither a standardized methodology for the simulation of an aircraft’s life cycle, nor a widely used standardized methodology for the estimation of their life cycle cost [Aircraft OEM, 2022]. Some best practices, although rather general in nature, can be found in SAE ARP 4293 and SAE AIR 1939. 2. Uncertainty Modeling Uncertainty in the life cycle simulation (including, but not limited to, future price developments, expected demands, and the environment) leads to inaccurate predictions and reduced trust in the overall benefit of predictive maintenance [Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022]. These uncertainties can be of aleatory nature (i.e., not reducible, as it stems from the system’s natural randomness) or of epistemic nature (i.e., potentially reducible, as it stems from a lack of knowledge). Difficulties include the

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: , airlines, OEMs, MROs, and other stakeholders have to compare the cost of adoption (Barrier 3) against the benefits such a program could provide. This Section deals with the estimation of the latter. 6.1 Challenges Estimating the impact of predictive maintenance in commercial aviation is a complex endeavor. Challenges that stakeholders identified include: 1. Life Cycle Cost Modeling There is a lack of accurate and reliable life cycle cost and risk modeling (Ref. 11, 32). [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022][Brian Tucker, Bell, 2022][Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Wes Ryan, Former FAA, 2023] Life cycle costs include acquisition, operation, maintenance, and expected future repair and replacement. Life cycle cost modeling help airlines understand the impact of PdM and assess if its cost are worth the benefits of reduced downtime and reduction of unforeseen repairs. 63% of companies not working on implementing PdM indicate that their reason for not doing so was because of their inability to quantify the impact/business case (Ref. 9). Without this data, it is difficult for OEMs to tailor aircraft component design (including sensor integration) and manufacturing to reduce maintenance costs. Risk modeling examines how maintenance techniques may affect aircraft safety and dependability. Without proper comprehension of the potential risks and the likelihood of their occurrence, airlines are hindered in making informed decisions regarding optimal maintenance strategies. To this date, there exists neither a standardized methodology for the simulation of an aircraft’s life cycle, nor a widely used standardized methodology for the estimation of their life cycle cost [Aircraft OEM, 2022]. Some best practices, although rather general in nature, can be found in SAE ARP 4293 and SAE AIR 1939. 2. Uncertainty Modeling Uncertainty in the life cycle simulation (including, but not limited to, future price developments, expected demands, and the environment) leads to inaccurate predictions and reduced trust in the overall benefit of predictive maintenance [Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022]. These uncertainties can be of aleatory nature (i.e., not reducible, as it stems from the system’s natural randomness) or of epistemic nature (i.e., potentially reducible, as it stems from a lack of knowledge). content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (

domain_applicability.reason: Nur Luftfahrt-Abkürzungen; keine allgemeinen Betriebs-/Instandhaltungsfaktoren beschrieben.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Ein Transfer von Wayside- zu Rolling-Stock-in-Motion-Sensorik ist wirtschaftlich/organisatorisch nur dann skalierbar, wenn für das neue Onboard-Sensorsystem belastbare Lebenszykluskosten- und Risikomodelle (Acquisition/Operation/Maintenance/Replacement sowie Safety/Dependability-Risiken) vorliegen, da fehlende standardisierte LCC-/Risikomodellierung die Quantifizierung des Business Case und damit die Adoptionsentscheidung verhindert.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Fit gut bzgl. ökonomischer/organisatorischer Skalierung: NASA-TM20230000841 nennt fehlende verlässliche LCC- und Risikomodellierung als zentrale Adoptionsbarriere (inkl. Sensorintegration) und berichtet, dass 63% den Business Case nicht quantifizieren können. Allerdings stammt Evidenz aus Aviation/PdM und belegt den konkreten Transfer Wayside→Onboard im Bahnkontext nur indirekt; keine Gegenbelege im Korpus.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Für funktionale Äquivalenz bei der Zustandsüberwachung (z.B. Hotbox/Wheels/Brakes) müssen Onboard-Lösungen eine Messleistung erreichen, die mindestens den im Wayside-Kontext genutzten Sensorparametern entspricht (z.B. Infrarotmessung mit integrierter Autokalibrierung, definierter Messfrequenz/Speed-Bereich sowie Umwelt-Temperaturbereich), sonst sinkt Detektionsqualität bei hohen Geschwindigkeiten und wechselnden Umweltbedingungen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Fit mittel-gut: ISSE 2009 liefert konkrete Wayside-Benchmark-Parameter (IR-Sensoren: Autokalibrierung, 250 Hz, bis 500 km/h, Ambient -30°C bis +50°C). Die Hypothese extrapoliert, dass Onboard mindestens diese Parameter erreichen muss; das ist plausibel als Referenz, aber nicht direkt belegt (keine Onboard-Vergleichsdaten, keine Nachweise zu Detektionsabfall bei Onboard).

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Eine zentrale Voraussetzung für den Transfer auf Rolling Stock in Motion ist eine datenreduzierende Edge-Verarbeitung mit standardisiertem, kompaktem Ergebnisformat (z.B. XML) und priorisierter Alarmübermittlung, da bereits Wayside-Systeme zur Minimierung der Übertragungsdatenmenge lokale Verarbeitung nutzen und Alarme mit Priorität an Betriebsstellen/Signalisierung senden.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Fit gut für die Aussage, dass Wayside-Systeme lokale Verarbeitung und kompakte Ergebnisübertragung nutzen: ISSE 2009 beschreibt lokale Verarbeitung (wenige Minuten nach Durchfahrt) und Versand von Ergebnissen/Alarmen via LAN/Internet/GSM-R; zur Datenmengenreduktion wird XML-Protokoll genutzt. Übertragung auf Onboard als Voraussetzung ist jedoch eine Generalisierung; Evidenz belegt primär Wayside-Architektur, nicht zwingend Onboard-Notwendigkeit.

breadth_expansion.expansion_reasoning: Need broader support because fit_score is below validation threshold.

falsification_attempt.reasoning: Fit gut für die Aussage, dass Wayside-Systeme lokale Verarbeitung und kompakte Ergebnisübertragung nutzen: ISSE 2009 beschreibt lokale Verarbeitung (wenige Minuten nach Durchfahrt) und Versand von Ergebnissen/Alarmen via LAN/Internet/GSM-R; zur Datenmengenreduktion wird XML-Protokoll genutzt. Übertragung auf Onboard als Voraussetzung ist jedoch eine Generalisierung; Evidenz belegt primär Wayside-Architektur, nicht zwingend Onboard-Notwendigkeit.

updated_hypotheses.statement: Die Übernahme streckenseitiger Identifikationsfunktionen in Richtung Rolling Stock erfordert robuste Identifikations-/Zuordnungsmechanismen (z.B. RFID), weil optische Verfahren (OCR) in realen Betriebsbedingungen (Verschmutzung/Zustand der Beschilderung, Geschwindigkeit, Wetter/Licht) unzuverlässig sind; ein Transfer muss daher eine technisch belastbare Fahrzeug-/Achszuordnung sicherstellen.
updated_hypotheses.reasoning: Fit gut: ISSE 2009 berichtet, dass OCR in realen Bedingungen (Schmutz/Rost, Geschwindigkeit, Wetter/Licht) unzuverlässig war und daher RFID (Rail-TAG/Rail-READER) gewählt wurde; zudem werden Spezifikationen inkl. sehr hoher Reading Accuracy und hoher Geschwindigkeitstauglichkeit genannt. Die Hypothese überträgt das auf die Notwendigkeit robuster Zuordnung beim Transfer; direkte Onboard-Identifikationsanforderungen werden nicht belegt, aber das Problem der zuverlässigen Fahrzeug-/Achszuordnung wird klar gestützt.

challenge_findings.description: Evidenz für LCC-/Risikomodell als Voraussetzung kommt aus Aviation-PdM und ist nur indirekt auf Bahn/Wayside→Onboard übertragbar; rail-spezifische Kosten-/RAMS-Standards oder Fallstudien fehlen im Korpus.

Es fehlen Onboard-Mess-/Detektionsdaten, um die Wayside-IR-Spezifikationen (250 Hz, Autokalibrierung, Ambient-Bereich, Speed-Bereich) als notwendige Mindestanforderung zu bestätigen; derzeit nur Wayside-Benchmark belegt.

keywords: onboard hotbox detection performance comparison wayside

challenge_findings.description: Die Datenreduktion per Edge/XML ist für Wayside nachgewiesen; für Onboard könnte alternativ höhere Bandbreite/andere Protokolle (z.B. komprimierte Binary, MQTT) ausreichend sein. Notwendigkeit 'standardisiert XML' ist daher eher Designwahl als harte Voraussetzung (im Korpus nicht entscheidbar).

keywords: onboard edge vs cloud bandwidth rolling stock

challenge_findings.description: OCR-basierte Identifikation ist unter realen Betriebsbedingungen unzuverlässig; RFID-basierte Identifikation wird als robustere Alternative beschrieben und unterstützt die Forderung nach belastbarer Zuordnung beim Transfer.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,
