content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: aining enough information on complete engine failures is a significant hurdle. It could take years for an engine such as turbofan engine to naturally degrade from its peak performance to a complete breakdown. In practical scenarios, experiencing failure is highly unfavorable as it poses safety risks and carries the potential for severe and irreversible damage. As a consequence, engine parts are replaced long before they reach the point of failure, prioritizing safety over the collection of complete degradation data. This lack of "run-to-failure" data, which is particularly valuable because it captures the engine's natural degradation process in its entirety creates a major challenge to for advanced machine accurately predict an engine's remaining useful life [29,33,35,38]. learning algorithms trying Existing reviews on aircraft engine health have highlighted the potential of machine learning, particularly for predicting RUL. The methods proposed by researchers often combine existing RUL models with for generating realistic time-series data. These AI networks excel at finding patterns in data, helping them understand the relationships between various sensor measurements. Additionally, they can learn from sequences, allowing them to capture how sensor readings change over time [35]. techniques 3.5.2 Rolling Bearings. Bearings minimize frictional forces between parts engaged in relative motion along a predetermined axis. For high-end equipment, condition monitoring and diagnosing bearing health is a critical aspect of PHM. In the aviation industry, high-speed bearings within an aeroengine's rotor system are undeniably crucial for ensuring the safe and stable operation of the aircraft. However, these bearings are susceptible to various faults due to the harsh environments they endure during extended operation. These faults can manifest as vibrations caused by rotor issues like misalignment, rubbing or loose components. Ultimately, bearing faults can lead to equipment failure, compromising sustained airworthiness, and potentially causing significant financial losses or even catastrophic accidents [28]. Vibration signals, due to their ease of use and wide frequency range, are a critical indicator of bearing health. However, diagnosing faults through these signals can be difficult. The complexity of bearing fault modes, such as cracks, wear, and lubrication problems, leads to vibration signals that are erratic, non-linear, and constantly changing. Aeroengine rotor systems present unique challenges for intelligent diagnostic methods. Rolling bearings in these systems operate continuously under harsh conditions – high speeds, high temperatures, and variable workloads. The complex combination of centrifugal, aerodynamic, and thermal [28]. A loads further complicates diagnosis significant hurdle stems from the imbalanced nature of the data. content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Smart Grid, such as CPSG, SGPS, USF, INEL and NREL which are listed in [113]. A proper testbed can help to find cyber security vulnerabilities, perform evaluations and execute verifications. As a result, designing a CBTC security testbed is significant to enhance the security level of train control systems and an efficient way to verify the availability of security technologies. According to the experiences of industrial control systems, there are three testbed constructing plans [114]: pure duplication, pure emulation and the hybrid way with du- plication and emulation. Obviously, the pure duplication way can provide the most comprehensive information of the targeting system and give the most real parameters of the targeting system under the security attacks. However, the best performance is achieved with the high costs as the equipment of the testbed is the same as that used in the real filed. Besides, the testbed built in the pure duplication way gen- erally covers a large area. For CBTC systems, a pure duplication testbed is hard to be built up as the train is running at the high speed and the operation environment is too large. On the other hand, the pure emu- lation way cannot describe the entire characteristics of CBTC systems as the fault-tolerance architecture, the safe logics and the fail-safe me- chanism are hard to be modeled. Therefore, the hybrid way with du- plication and emulation is more proper to develop the CBTC security testbed. As mentioned above, CBTC system is data-driven which means in- formation flow is the most important to the system. Therefore, ob- taining the detailed data generation principles, the transmission me- chanisms and the data processing logics are significant to determine the threats and the vulnerabilities of the system. As a result, according to the data evolution process, the critical devices or subsystems should be duplicated with the same ones as the practical operation lines. As CBTC is a typical cyber-physical system, it contains two major components, a physical process and a cyber system [115]. Based on the features and architecture of CBTC systems, except the rolling stock, almost all the other train control equipment belongs to the cyber system. From Fig. 2, the TDCS, ATS/CTC, VC/VOBC, RBC/ZC, CI are the critical parts, which means the CBTC security testbed should adopt the real signalling equipment. However, the train can use an emulation product, which can generate the kinetic model and provide the

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.title: On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zur Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge – Status quo und Systemtypologie.
content.abstract_or_snippet: variablen Betrag in Abhängigkeit von der derzeitigen Nachfrage, des Wochentags und der Anzahl gebuchter Personen. Abbildung 23: Ansichten des Innenraums eines MOIA-Fahrzeuges Quelle: Schatzmann et al., 2023, S. 9. MOIA ist auch deswegen ein Musterbeispiel dieses Typs, weil das Unternehmen eine Tochter des Konzerns Volkswagen ist. Mit seiner Flotte von über 565 Fahr- zeugen in Hamburg und Hannover handelt es sich bei MOIA um die größte Ride- pooling-Flotte in Europa (Zwick, 2023). Hier testet ein Automobilgigant, ob und wie in Zukunft nicht mehr Autos, sondern Automobildienstleistungen verkauft werden können. 72 | Wuppertal Institut 202_Wuppertal Paper On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zu Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge Daseinsvorsorge und ökologische Nachhaltigkeit: Komfortshuttles zielen auf ein neues Geschäftsmodell durch einen Wandel vom Besitz zur Nutzung von Autos. Aufgrund des hohen Komfortniveaus und des nahezu 24/7 verfügbaren Services ist es gut dazu geeignet, wohlhabenden Autobesitzer*innen in Großstädten einen voll- wertigen Ersatz für ihren PKW zu bieten. Eine Substitution von Fahrten mit Bus und Bahn bzw. Fahrrad auf Komfortshuttles ist aufgrund des relativ hohen Preises eher in geringerem Umfang zu erwarten. Substitutionen von Taxis und Ridehailing sind hingegen wahrscheinlich. Für die adressierten Personengruppen, die tendenziell hohe Ausgaben für ihre PKW haben und oft lange nach Parkplätzen suchen müssen, können sich finanzielle Ersparnisse und funktionale Vorteile gegenüber privater Au- tomobilität ergeben. Dies könnte durch eine Abschaffung des Dienstwagenprivilegs noch verstärkt werden. Allgemein gilt: Mit steigender Nutzung und daher auch wach- sender Flotte, sinken die Kosten je Fahrgast und höhere Poolingraten können mit geringeren Umwegen erreicht werden.

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,

In addition to using vehicle-to-vehicle communications such as V2V (vehicle to vehicle) and V2I (vehicle to infrastructure), autonomous vehicles also analyze and integrate various sensor data to perceive the external environment. Currently, the commonly used sensor types include radar, camera (including RGB-D, infrared cam- era), ultrasonic, lidar, and GNSS/IMU. Because the detection capabilities and reliability of various sensors in different environments are limited, the fusion of multi-sensor data can improve the detection and recognition accuracy. Taking local conditions and the remoteness of a check-point, there are basically two methods for transferring data – by the fiber-optic network of NRIC (wired) or by the GSM-R technology (wirelessly). The locations of the check-points throughout the railway in Bulgaria are shown on Fig. 1. into account the in Bulgaria will feature For reasons of economy and efficiency the wayside monitoring system the following functions: 1) Identification of a vehicle by its individual number; 2) Measuring the load of each wheel – detection of overload, unbalanced vehicles and uneven load distribution between the wheels and axles; 3) Detection of wheel defects – flat spots, out of roundness, polygonizaition, etc.; 4) Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes; 5) Telecommunication between the local check-points and control centers. Basically every vehicle check-point identification and telecommunication. But these two features alone could not deliver useful information about some of the parameters of the passing trains. This demands at least one more feature to be added, e.g. detection of wheel load, detection of wheel defects or detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes. featuring be 3.1. Vehicle identification microsystem The vehicle identification microsystem is crucial for the wayside monitoring system as all of the measured parameters of the passing train are linked to each vehicle of it. For that reason, such a microsystem should have the ability to work reliably at any environment conditions. The microsystem should also be able to accurately read the vehicle’s identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. During the decision making about what type of identification method should the microsystems is best to use, the research team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). Dabei kann es sich beispielsweise um übermittelte Sensordaten, durchgeführte Servicearbeiten oder Produktionsdaten handeln. Der Digitale Master und Digitale Schatten bilden zusammen die Datenbasis des Digitalen Zwillings� Laut einer Studie des Fraunhofer IPK und msg bestehen Digitale Zwillinge aus drei Komponenten [22]. Die erste Komponente bildet der »Digitale Master«, der die Stammdaten des zuge- hörigen Objekts als auch alle Modelle und Informationen aus der Planungsphase beinhaltet. Der Digitale Master kann zusätzlich zu grundlegenden Objektinformationen, wie Objekt- typ (z.B. Motor) und Eigenschaften (Gewicht, Herstellungsjahr), auch Materialstücklisten oder CAD3-Modelle umfassen. Die zweite Komponente bildet der »Digitale Schatten«. Dieser bein- haltet hauptsächlich Betriebs-, Prozess- und Zustandsdaten Die dritte, für die Wertschöpfung zentrale, Komponente bildet die Verknüpfung von Informationen aus Digitalem Master und Digitalem Schatten, um Lösungen für spezifische Anwen- dungsfälle zu schaffen [22]. Während sich Digitale Zwillinge branchenübergreifend häufig in der Hinsicht ähneln, dass sowohl Digitale Master- und Digitale Schattendaten bereit- gestellt und verknüpft werden, sind die darauf aufbauenden Anwendungen sehr vielfältig im Hinblick auf ihre Art und Kom- plexität. Abbildung 2 stellt beispielhaft die Komponenten eines Digitalen Zwillings für einen Motor schematisch dar. Plank-Wiedenbeck Erstellt am: 16.01.2011 | Stand des Wissens: 16.04.2025 Für moderne Verkehrstelematiksysteme ist zunehmend die Vernetzung und die damit einhergehende schnelle Datenübertragung von zentraler Bedeutung. Daten und die darin enthaltenen Informationen sollen möglichst ohne Zeitverzug vom Erfassungsort (Sensorik, fest installiert oder mobil) zur (Verkehrs-)Zentrale, von der Zentrale zum Verkehrsteilnehmenden oder von Verkehrsteilnehmenden zu Verkehrsteilnehmenden (beispielweise bei der Fahrzeug-zu-Fahrzeug- Kommunikation (Car-to-Car-Communication, C2C) übertragen werden. Systeme, die diese Informationen als Eingangsdaten nutzen, können so zeitnah Aussagen, beispielsweise über den aktuellen Zustand eines Verkehrssystems, liefern. Im Folgenden ist eine Übersicht der gebräuchlichsten Übertragungstechnologien zusammengestellt, aufgeschlüsselt nach maximal zu erreichender Datenrate und den entstehenden Kosten [Los18]: Auszug aus http://www.forschungsinformationssystem.de vom 12.02.2026 Seite 1 ● ● ● Synthesebericht: Techniken der Datenübertragung für Intelligente Verkehrssysteme EintragsID: 339652 Tabelle 1: Übersicht der ÜbertragungsmethodenÜbertragungsmethoden (Grafik zum Vergrößern bitte anklicken) Die leitungsgebundene Übertragung bietet insbesondere durch die Verwendung von Glasfasertechnologie hohe Datenübertragungsraten. content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. BASIC PRINCIPLES AND APPROACHES FOR MODELLING OF THE SYSTEM The modelling process and building of a wayside monitoring system includes many factors that need to be taken into account, such as: basic functions of the monitoring system, choosing the location of the check-points, selecting the right method for vehicle identification, appropriate measuring methods and sensors, defining procedures and algorithms about each local check-point, processing statistical information about alarms, etc. selecting In order the information obtained through the monitoring system to be useful for real-time decision making and the vehicles and loads to be tracked,

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - 5G network planning in connecting urban areas for trains service using a genetic algorithm
content.abstract_or_snippet: efficiency and interference levels. Its steady fitness improvement and adaptability make it well-suited for scenarios where wide coverage is a priority alongside acceptable performance trade-offs. 1. Introduction High speed trains need to offer seamless connectivity and Quality of Service (QoS) to the passengers in order to have a satisfactory ex- perience during their journey. The necessity for optimal capacity and increased data rates is evident since there is a large number of users that are connected to the telecommunications network that is in operation. The growth in mobile data traffic is primarily driven by the global rise in mobile broadband subscriptions, alongside an increase in average data consumption per user. This surge is largely attributed to the streaming and sharing of high-definition videos and the growing adoption of immersive video content, which is often the case with passengers in high speed and long journey trains. A recent report pre- dicts that global mobile data traffic will quadruple between 2019 and 2025, reaching 160 exabytes per month, with an additional 53 exabytes per month expected from fixed wireless access traffic [1]. These pro- jections, combined with the diverse requirements of emerging servi- ces—such as low latency and high reliability. In particular, in rail communications 5G emerges [2] as a new communication paradigm dedicated to railways. From Global System for Mobile Communications-Railway (GSM-R) to Long Term Evolution- Railway (LTE-R) [3] rail communications are continuously advancing reaching 5G-R [4]. In such a way, bottlenecks of the communications in trains are addressed by introducing the advantages of 5G communica- tion to the passengers such as increased capacity, low latency, and higher throughput. Industry players such as mobile operators and equipment vendors are already aggressively trialing and deploying 5G-R technologies to meet evolving connectivity demands. The rapid changes in mobile traffic patterns and speed of high speed trains highlight the need for ⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: espyrou@certh.gr (E.D. content.title: Synthesebericht Techniken der Datenubertragung fur Intelligente Verkehrssysteme
content.abstract_or_snippet: übertragungsraten. Allerdings ist der Aufwand für das Verlegen von Leitungen und die damit verbundenen Kosten hoch. Werden bereits bestehende Infrastrukturkomponenten benutzt, beispielsweise öffentliche Internet- basierte Netze, so fallen die Kosten deutlich geringer aus. Ein besonderer Fokus muss hier jedoch auf das Sicherheitsmanagement gelegt werden. Dies kann durch Einrichtung eines Virtuellen Privaten Netzwerks (VPN) erfolgen. Die drahtlose Übertragung bietet sich für mobile, sogenannte "Floating Car Data" (FCD), oder temporär installierte Sensorik sowie zur drahtlosen Anbindung von Sensoren an Streckenstationen an und lässt sich meist kostengünstiger installieren, da keine Leitungen verlegt oder gemietet werden müssen. Nachteile sind die freie Zugänglichkeit des Netzes, die Einschränkung der Reichweite der Funkübertragung sowie deren Beeinflussung durch vorherrschende Umfeldbedingungen. Prinzipiell kann jeder, der ein entsprechendes Empfangsgerät besitzt "mithören", was auf der Leitung passiert. Ein funktionierendes Sicherheitsmanagement ist Auszug aus http://www.forschungsinformationssystem.de vom 12.02.2026 Seite 2 Synthesebericht: Techniken der Datenübertragung für Intelligente Verkehrssysteme EintragsID: 339652 daher Grundvoraussetzung für den sicheren Betrieb sowie ein umfangreicheres Fehlermanagement, um mögliche Störungen erkennen und beheben zu können. Bei den bisher aufgeführten Übertragungstechnologien handelt es sich meist um Technologien zur Punkt-zu- Punkt-Verbindung zweier Kommunikationspartner. Es ist aber möglich nach weiteren Kommunikationsformen und Verbindungsarten zu unterscheiden. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf und konfigurieren sich selbständig. Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten. In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen, Verkehrsprognosen und Umfahrungsempfehlungen ableiten. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - 5G network planning in connecting urban areas for trains service using a genetic algorithm
content.abstract_or_snippet: 2025 2949-8678/© 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http:// creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). E.D. Spyrou and V. Kappatos High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 155–162 networks that can scale for both expected and unforeseen scenarios. 5G- R is envisioned as a unified connectivity solution capable of handling growing data traffic and supporting diverse rail-based use cases. In general, 5G requires both advancements in core networks (e.g., network slicing, virtualization) and the development of the 5G New Radio (NR) air interface. This includes improvements in spectral efficiency, support for higher-order modulation, massive MIMO, and the use of millimeter wave frequencies. Additionally, 5G enhances network capacity through spectrum reuse and network densification, which involves increasing the number of cell sites, often small cells, to supplement existing macrocellular networks [5]. The deployment of 5G services requires the installation of new 5G infrastructure, known as next-generation Node-B (gNB), across the target area. Something similar is necessary for 5G-R installations. The process of selecting and configuring the locations where this 5G equipment will be installed is commonly referred to as 5G cellular planning [6]. This is a complex task that involves balancing factors such as costs, coverage area, and Electro-Magnetic Field (EMF) regulations. In high speed train scenarios, the installations should take place taking into consideration the speed and coverage while ensuring QoS. 5G-R which should be a standard at a European level as well, is a dedicated version of the 5G network that addresses the specific requirements of railway operations. It emphasizes ultra-reliable, low-latency commu- nication, which is essential for real-time train control, monitoring, and ensuring safety. Additionally, it supports applications like predictive maintenance, trackside surveillance, and passenger information sys- tems. While 5G-R is primarily optimized for railway use, its feature- s—such as high reliability, low latency, and large data capacity—can also cater to certain User Equipment (UE) needs, including real-time streaming, Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity, and remote control applications. However, these capabilities are generally designed with the operational demands of the railway industry in mind, rather

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: team had made several trials of different available systems for optical character recognition (OCR). The results were unsatisfactory for several reason. The major effect on the OCR was that the identification numbers, plates, signs, etc. of the vehicles are not kept in good condition. Most of the freight vehicles were covered with dirt, rust, etc. The speed of the passing train was also a problem for accurate OCR. Adding the weather conditions, such as raining, snowing, sun light, etc., that made the OCR method for vehicles identification inapplicable. Based on the results from the OCR trials, the research team has decided to use radio frequency devices to transmit data between each other as the train passes the check-point – a Rail-TAG and a Rail- READER. The Rail-TAG represents an electronic device, mounted on the vehicle, which stores the vehicles identification data – Fig. 2 [1]. The specifications of the Rail-TAG transponder are listed in Table 1 [1]. Fig. 2. Rail-TAG transponder, mounted on a wagon. Fig. 3. Rail-READER, mounted near the track. The Rail-READER – Fig. 3 [1] – is a wayside electronic device, which reads the data, transmitted by the Rail-TAG. The Rail-READER is the device, connected to the local check-point infrastructure. The specifications of the Rail-READER are listed in Table 2 [1]. Fig. 4 [1] represents the information flow from the Rail-TAGs, through the Rail-READER and to the wayside infrastructure. Fig. 4. RFID information flow. Table 1. Rail-TAG transponder specifications. Type RAIL-TAG Size Weight Mounting Location Frequency Energy supply Lifetime Power consumption Reading distance Certificates Reading accuracy Applicable speeds Temperature 130x28x20 mm (LxWxH) 50 gr 5 mm screw of gluing According TSI, right+left 868 MHz (UHF) Internal > 6 years < 1 mW Up to 50 m CE (EN300220-1); ETSI EN3001489-1 Better than 99.9% Up to 230 km/h -30°C to +70°C Table 2. Rail-READER specifications. content.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Basically the force sensor represents a 1140mm long P49E1 rail section with milled surfaces (sensitive areas) on which a total of four installed. The rosettes connection of the rosettes is shown on Fig. 5. strain-gauge are Fig. 5. Full bridge connection of strain-gauge rosettes. As the output signal of the tensometric bridges is of very small amplitude, a differential instrumental amplifier was included [2]. (standby) The sensor features two power modes – Low Power Mode and Normal Mode (measuring). The switching between the two modes is achieved by using rail contacts, installed on the track before and after the check-point measuring zone. The rail contacts also serve as axle counters. The electronics of the specialized force sensor is contained in a metal box, mounted on the rail section (Fig. 6). Specifications of the specialized force sensor are listed in Table 3. Table 3. content.title: WIK Diskussionsbeitrag Nr 315 01
content.abstract_or_snippet: I-Schicht 2) z.B. ATM-, Ethernet-, oder WDM-basiert sein kann. QoS kann komplementär zu den drei Strate- gien auch auf tieferen OSI-Schichten unterstützt werden. In dieser Studie werden daher QoS bei Ethernet und Multi Protocol Label Switching (MPLS) sowie in kürzerer Form ATM berücksichtigt. Abbildung 2-2 ordnet alle Verfahren in das OSI-Schichtenmodell ein. Abbildung 2-2: Systematisierung der QoS-Strategien anhand des ISO/OSI bzw. TCP/IP Schichtenmodells OSI Schicht TCP/IP Schicht Protokoll Instrumente/ Strategien Application (Anwendungsschicht) Presentation (Darstellungsschicht) Session (Sitzungs- bzw. Kommunikationsschicht) t r e i t n e i r o s g n u d n e w n A Transport (Transportschicht) Network (Netzwerk- bzw. Vermittlungsschicht) Data Link (Sicherungsschicht) t r e i t n e i r o t r o p s n a r T Anwendung Transport Internet Physical (Bitübertragungsschicht) Netzzugang 7 6 5 4 3 2 1 Quelle: WIK HTTP FTP HTTPS NCP TCP UDP SPX RSVP ICMP IGMP IP IPX Ethernet ATM Frame Relay SDH WDM IntServ über RSVP DiffServ Kennzeichnung im IP-Header IEEE 802.1q Implementierung im Ethernet-Frame MPLS Strategien zur Realisierung von Quality of Service in IP-Netzen 9 Im Anschluss werden die drei wesentlichen Realisierungsstrategien für QoS in IP Net- zen beschrieben (Kapitel 2.1.1 - 2.1.2) und danach hinsichtlich ihrer Stärken und Schwächen verglichen (Kapitel 2.2). Anschließend werden die komplementären Ansät- ze zu QoS mit Ethernet und MPLS in Kapitel 2.3 sowie die bisher zur QoS Implementie- rung vorherrschende ATM-Technik beschrieben. content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: , airlines, OEMs, MROs, and other stakeholders have to compare the cost of adoption (Barrier 3) against the benefits such a program could provide. This Section deals with the estimation of the latter. 6.1 Challenges Estimating the impact of predictive maintenance in commercial aviation is a complex endeavor. Challenges that stakeholders identified include: 1. Life Cycle Cost Modeling There is a lack of accurate and reliable life cycle cost and risk modeling (Ref. 11, 32). [Darren Macer, Boeing, 2023][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022][Brian Tucker, Bell, 2022][Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Wes Ryan, Former FAA, 2023] Life cycle costs include acquisition, operation, maintenance, and expected future repair and replacement. Life cycle cost modeling help airlines understand the impact of PdM and assess if its cost are worth the benefits of reduced downtime and reduction of unforeseen repairs. 63% of companies not working on implementing PdM indicate that their reason for not doing so was because of their inability to quantify the impact/business case (Ref. 9). Without this data, it is difficult for OEMs to tailor aircraft component design (including sensor integration) and manufacturing to reduce maintenance costs. Risk modeling examines how maintenance techniques may affect aircraft safety and dependability. Without proper comprehension of the potential risks and the likelihood of their occurrence, airlines are hindered in making informed decisions regarding optimal maintenance strategies. To this date, there exists neither a standardized methodology for the simulation of an aircraft’s life cycle, nor a widely used standardized methodology for the estimation of their life cycle cost [Aircraft OEM, 2022]. Some best practices, although rather general in nature, can be found in SAE ARP 4293 and SAE AIR 1939. 2. Uncertainty Modeling Uncertainty in the life cycle simulation (including, but not limited to, future price developments, expected demands, and the environment) leads to inaccurate predictions and reduced trust in the overall benefit of predictive maintenance [Kai Wicke, DLR, 2022][Kai Goebel, PARC, 2022]. These uncertainties can be of aleatory nature (i.e., not reducible, as it stems from the system’s natural randomness) or of epistemic nature (i.e., potentially reducible, as it stems from a lack of knowledge). Difficulties include the

content.title: NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript
content.abstract_or_snippet: invest to overcome them, aiming to support the scaled adoption of predictive and prescriptive maintenance in aviation. Abbreviations AHM Aircraft Health Management CAA Civilian Aviation Authority CBM Condition Based Maintenance CBM+ Condition Based Maintenance Plus DoE Department of Energy FAA Federal Aviation Administration HUMS Health and Usage Monitoring System IMRBPB International Maintenance Review Board Policy Board OEM Original Equipment Manufacturer MEL Minimum Equipment List MRB Maintenance Review Board MRBR Maintenance Review Board Report MRO Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul MPIG Maintenance Programs Industry Group PdM Predictive Maintenance PHM Prognostics and Health Management PIML Physics Informed Machine Learning RCM Reliability-Centered Maintenance SME Subject Matter Experts 1 Background 1.1 Maintenance Strategies At a high level, airlines and operators craft maintenance poli- cies to minimize costs while ensuring a required level of safety. A spectrum of different maintenance policies can be applied to achieve these goals. An operator’s maintenance policy fre- quently consists of some combination of strategies according to data availability, failure risk, and consequence of failure. Maintenance policies generally fall into two overarching cat- egories: corrective and preventative (Ref. 1). For corrective maintenance (also called reactive maintenance), a failure trig- gers maintenance. This is common for non-safety critical systems where the consequence of failure is low (e.g., a coffee machine). This is occasionally referred to as run to failure. Alternatively, preventative maintenance strategies are designed to schedule maintenance before a failure occurs. Preventative maintenance strategies are further divided into two categories: rule-based and indicator-based. In rule-based maintenance, pre-defined rules trigger maintenance based on Figure 1.—Hierarchy of Main- tenance Strategies time, usage, or risk. Generally, these rules are defined using a reliability analysis, called Reliability- Centered Maintenance (RCM), where a distribution of failure times is determined experimentally as a function of time, usage, or risk. A distribution percentile is selected based on the program’s risk tolerance. For example, choosing the first percentile indicates a willingness to accept a 1% risk of failure. Maintenance will then be performed on the selected fixed interval. Most modern maintenance programs can be categorized as some form of RCM (Ref. 2). Rule-based approaches naturally result in unnecessary maintenance. The amount of unnecessary maintenance is dependent on the operator’s risk tolerance. A lower accepted risk of failure results in lower costs from line (i.e., gate) maintenance and associated downtime at the cost of greater unnecessary base (
content.url_or_identifier: /app/data/eval_dataset/docs/NASA-TM20230000841 - An Analysis of Barriers Preventing the Widespread Adoption of Predictive and Prescript.pdf

content.title: ISSE 2009 - Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion
content.abstract_or_snippet: Sensors and Microsystems of Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion Nencho Nenov1), Emil Dimitrov2), Nikolay Dodev1) and Vasil Vasilev1) 1) Department of Transport Equipment, University of Transport, Sofia, Bulgaria 2) Department of Electronic Technology, Technical University of Sofia, Bulgaria nnenov_58@abv.bg Abstract: The paper presented is dedicated to the development and application of sensors and microsystems for evaluating various parameters of rolling stock in motion. The intended purpose of such a system, comprising of the described sensors and microsystems and also known as Wayside Monitoring System for Rolling Stock in Motion, is to identify the current overall condition of the railway vehicles, including hot boxes (axle bearings) and brakes (wheel brakes and disc brakes), wheel defects (flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc), dynamic weighing and load distribution, etc. All of these could compromise the safety of the railroad traffic, leading to accidents such as vehicle derailment and increased wear and damages of the tracks. 1. INTRODUCTION The working condition under which the railway vehicles operate could be very severe. This in most cases includes innumerable elements and activities that have a detrimental effect on its overall condition and potentially on the safety of passengers and freight. The capacity and speeds of the rolling stock also increases. In order to be cost effective, the railway traffic should be running smooth and free of accidents and unscheduled track maintenance. of In order to evaluate the overall condition of the rolling stock without interrupting the railway work, the research team is currently developing sensors and microsystems of wayside monitoring system for the needs the Bulgarian National Railway Infrastructure Company (NRIC). The monitoring system will be also used by the railway operators and haulers in order to track their vehicles and loads. Another intended purpose of the system is the economic effect by increasing the collection of freight charges of overloaded vehicles. 2. content.title: Nasim - Generative AI based Predictive Maintenance in Aviation A Systematic Literature Review
content.abstract_or_snippet: aining enough information on complete engine failures is a significant hurdle. It could take years for an engine such as turbofan engine to naturally degrade from its peak performance to a complete breakdown. In practical scenarios, experiencing failure is highly unfavorable as it poses safety risks and carries the potential for severe and irreversible damage. As a consequence, engine parts are replaced long before they reach the point of failure, prioritizing safety over the collection of complete degradation data. This lack of "run-to-failure" data, which is particularly valuable because it captures the engine's natural degradation process in its entirety creates a major challenge to for advanced machine accurately predict an engine's remaining useful life [29,33,35,38]. learning algorithms trying Existing reviews on aircraft engine health have highlighted the potential of machine learning, particularly for predicting RUL. The methods proposed by researchers often combine existing RUL models with for generating realistic time-series data. These AI networks excel at finding patterns in data, helping them understand the relationships between various sensor measurements. Additionally, they can learn from sequences, allowing them to capture how sensor readings change over time [35]. techniques 3.5.2 Rolling Bearings. Bearings minimize frictional forces between parts engaged in relative motion along a predetermined axis. For high-end equipment, condition monitoring and diagnosing bearing health is a critical aspect of PHM. In the aviation industry, high-speed bearings within an aeroengine's rotor system are undeniably crucial for ensuring the safe and stable operation of the aircraft. However, these bearings are susceptible to various faults due to the harsh environments they endure during extended operation. These faults can manifest as vibrations caused by rotor issues like misalignment, rubbing or loose components. Ultimately, bearing faults can lead to equipment failure, compromising sustained airworthiness, and potentially causing significant financial losses or even catastrophic accidents [28]. Vibration signals, due to their ease of use and wide frequency range, are a critical indicator of bearing health. However, diagnosing faults through these signals can be difficult. The complexity of bearing fault modes, such as cracks, wear, and lubrication problems, leads to vibration signals that are erratic, non-linear, and constantly changing. Aeroengine rotor systems present unique challenges for intelligent diagnostic methods. Rolling bearings in these systems operate continuously under harsh conditions – high speeds, high temperatures, and variable workloads. The complex combination of centrifugal, aerodynamic, and thermal [28]. A loads further complicates diagnosis significant hurdle stems from the imbalanced nature of the data. content.title: High-speed Railway 1 - Security of railway control systems  A survey, research issues and challenges
content.abstract_or_snippet: Smart Grid, such as CPSG, SGPS, USF, INEL and NREL which are listed in [113]. A proper testbed can help to find cyber security vulnerabilities, perform evaluations and execute verifications. As a result, designing a CBTC security testbed is significant to enhance the security level of train control systems and an efficient way to verify the availability of security technologies. According to the experiences of industrial control systems, there are three testbed constructing plans [114]: pure duplication, pure emulation and the hybrid way with du- plication and emulation. Obviously, the pure duplication way can provide the most comprehensive information of the targeting system and give the most real parameters of the targeting system under the security attacks. However, the best performance is achieved with the high costs as the equipment of the testbed is the same as that used in the real filed. Besides, the testbed built in the pure duplication way gen- erally covers a large area. For CBTC systems, a pure duplication testbed is hard to be built up as the train is running at the high speed and the operation environment is too large. On the other hand, the pure emu- lation way cannot describe the entire characteristics of CBTC systems as the fault-tolerance architecture, the safe logics and the fail-safe me- chanism are hard to be modeled. Therefore, the hybrid way with du- plication and emulation is more proper to develop the CBTC security testbed. As mentioned above, CBTC system is data-driven which means in- formation flow is the most important to the system. Therefore, ob- taining the detailed data generation principles, the transmission me- chanisms and the data processing logics are significant to determine the threats and the vulnerabilities of the system. As a result, according to the data evolution process, the critical devices or subsystems should be duplicated with the same ones as the practical operation lines. As CBTC is a typical cyber-physical system, it contains two major components, a physical process and a cyber system [115]. Based on the features and architecture of CBTC systems, except the rolling stock, almost all the other train control equipment belongs to the cyber system. From Fig. 2, the TDCS, ATS/CTC, VC/VOBC, RBC/ZC, CI are the critical parts, which means the CBTC security testbed should adopt the real signalling equipment. However, the train can use an emulation product, which can generate the kinetic model and provide the

Mit seiner Flotte von über 565 Fahr- zeugen in Hamburg und Hannover handelt es sich bei MOIA um die größte Ride- pooling-Flotte in Europa (Zwick, 2023). Hier testet ein Automobilgigant, ob und wie in Zukunft nicht mehr Autos, sondern Automobildienstleistungen verkauft werden können. 72 | Wuppertal Institut 202_Wuppertal Paper On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zu Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge Daseinsvorsorge und ökologische Nachhaltigkeit: Komfortshuttles zielen auf ein neues Geschäftsmodell durch einen Wandel vom Besitz zur Nutzung von Autos. Aufgrund des hohen Komfortniveaus und des nahezu 24/7 verfügbaren Services ist es gut dazu geeignet, wohlhabenden Autobesitzer*innen in Großstädten einen voll- wertigen Ersatz für ihren PKW zu bieten. Eine Substitution von Fahrten mit Bus und Bahn bzw. Fahrrad auf Komfortshuttles ist aufgrund des relativ hohen Preises eher in geringerem Umfang zu erwarten. Substitutionen von Taxis und Ridehailing sind hingegen wahrscheinlich. Für die adressierten Personengruppen, die tendenziell hohe Ausgaben für ihre PKW haben und oft lange nach Parkplätzen suchen müssen, können sich finanzielle Ersparnisse und funktionale Vorteile gegenüber privater Au- tomobilität ergeben. Dies könnte durch eine Abschaffung des Dienstwagenprivilegs noch verstärkt werden. Allgemein gilt: Mit steigender Nutzung und daher auch wach- sender Flotte, sinken die Kosten je Fahrgast und höhere Poolingraten können mit geringeren Umwegen erreicht werden.

Dabei kann es sich beispielsweise um übermittelte Sensordaten, durchgeführte Servicearbeiten oder Produktionsdaten handeln. Der Digitale Master und Digitale Schatten bilden zusammen die Datenbasis des Digitalen Zwillings� Laut einer Studie des Fraunhofer IPK und msg bestehen Digitale Zwillinge aus drei Komponenten [22]. Die erste Komponente bildet der »Digitale Master«, der die Stammdaten des zuge- hörigen Objekts als auch alle Modelle und Informationen aus der Planungsphase beinhaltet. Der Digitale Master kann zusätzlich zu grundlegenden Objektinformationen, wie Objekt- typ (z.B. Motor) und Eigenschaften (Gewicht, Herstellungsjahr), auch Materialstücklisten oder CAD3-Modelle umfassen. Die zweite Komponente bildet der »Digitale Schatten«. Dieser bein- haltet hauptsächlich Betriebs-, Prozess- und Zustandsdaten Die dritte, für die Wertschöpfung zentrale, Komponente bildet die Verknüpfung von Informationen aus Digitalem Master und Digitalem Schatten, um Lösungen für spezifische Anwen- dungsfälle zu schaffen [22]. Während sich Digitale Zwillinge branchenübergreifend häufig in der Hinsicht ähneln, dass sowohl Digitale Master- und Digitale Schattendaten bereit- gestellt und verknüpft werden, sind die darauf aufbauenden Anwendungen sehr vielfältig im Hinblick auf ihre Art und Kom- plexität. Abbildung 2 stellt beispielhaft die Komponenten eines Digitalen Zwillings für einen Motor schematisch dar. Digitaler Zwilling Intelligente Verknüpfung • Zustandsüberwachung • Objektlokalisierung • Wartungsunterstützung • Produktoptimierung • ... Digitaler Master • Motorstammdaten • CAD Modell • Materialstücklisten • Produktionsdaten • Informationen der Inbetriebnahme • ... Digitaler Schatten • Betriebs

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - 5G network planning in connecting urban areas for trains service using a genetic algorithm
content.abstract_or_snippet: efficiency and interference levels. Its steady fitness improvement and adaptability make it well-suited for scenarios where wide coverage is a priority alongside acceptable performance trade-offs. 1. Introduction High speed trains need to offer seamless connectivity and Quality of Service (QoS) to the passengers in order to have a satisfactory ex- perience during their journey. The necessity for optimal capacity and increased data rates is evident since there is a large number of users that are connected to the telecommunications network that is in operation. The growth in mobile data traffic is primarily driven by the global rise in mobile broadband subscriptions, alongside an increase in average data consumption per user. This surge is largely attributed to the streaming and sharing of high-definition videos and the growing adoption of immersive video content, which is often the case with passengers in high speed and long journey trains. A recent report pre- dicts that global mobile data traffic will quadruple between 2019 and 2025, reaching 160 exabytes per month, with an additional 53 exabytes per month expected from fixed wireless access traffic [1]. These pro- jections, combined with the diverse requirements of emerging servi- ces—such as low latency and high reliability. In particular, in rail communications 5G emerges [2] as a new communication paradigm dedicated to railways. From Global System for Mobile Communications-Railway (GSM-R) to Long Term Evolution- Railway (LTE-R) [3] rail communications are continuously advancing reaching 5G-R [4]. In such a way, bottlenecks of the communications in trains are addressed by introducing the advantages of 5G communica- tion to the passengers such as increased capacity, low latency, and higher throughput. Industry players such as mobile operators and equipment vendors are already aggressively trialing and deploying 5G-R technologies to meet evolving connectivity demands. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf und konfigurieren sich selbständig. Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten. In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen, Verkehrsprognosen und Umfahrungsempfehlungen ableiten. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - 5G network planning in connecting urban areas for trains service using a genetic algorithm
content.abstract_or_snippet: 2025 2949-8678/© 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC BY license (http:// creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). E.D. Spyrou and V. Kappatos High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 155–162 networks that can scale for both expected and unforeseen scenarios. 5G- R is envisioned as a unified connectivity solution capable of handling growing data traffic and supporting diverse rail-based use cases. In general, 5G requires both advancements in core networks (e.g., network slicing, virtualization) and the development of the 5G New Radio (NR) air interface. This includes improvements in spectral efficiency, support for higher-order modulation, massive MIMO, and the use of millimeter wave frequencies. Additionally, 5G enhances network capacity through spectrum reuse and network densification, which involves increasing the number of cell sites, often small cells, to supplement existing macrocellular networks [5]. The deployment of 5G services requires the installation of new 5G infrastructure, known as next-generation Node-B (gNB), across the target area. Something similar is necessary for 5G-R installations. The process of selecting and configuring the locations where this 5G equipment will be installed is commonly referred to as 5G cellular planning [6]. This is a complex task that involves balancing factors such as costs, coverage area, and Electro-Magnetic Field (EMF) regulations. In high speed train scenarios, the installations should take place taking into consideration the speed and coverage while ensuring QoS. 5G-R which should be a standard at a European level as well, is a dedicated version of the 5G network that addresses the specific requirements of railway operations. It emphasizes ultra-reliable, low-latency commu- nication, which is essential for real-time train control, monitoring, and ensuring safety. Additionally, it supports applications like predictive maintenance, trackside surveillance, and passenger information sys- tems. While 5G-R is primarily optimized for railway use, its feature- s—such as high reliability, low latency, and large data capacity—can also cater to certain User Equipment (UE) needs, including real-time streaming, Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity, and remote control applications. However, these capabilities are generally designed with the operational demands of the railway industry in mind, rather

Der Datenraum ist als maritime Version des Internatio- nal Data Space (IDS) für den Schiffsbetrieb konzeptioniert, sodass eine Interoperabilität und Konnektivität gewährleistet ist, unter klarer Regulierung der Datenhoheit. Auf europäi- scher Ebene werden diese Themen ebenfalls adressiert und im Rahmen der Ozean Dekade Aktivitäten eine Vernetzung von Digital Twins of the Ocean über DITTO [33] ermöglicht. Ein dazu passender Ocean Data Space wird aktuell vom Konsortium von ILIAD: Integrated DigitaL Twins For Marine and MaritIme DAta AnD Information Services im Rahmen der Horizon 2020 Strategie mit Partnern wie Alpha Unmanned Systems, Thales Group, DEIMOS Eng, NextGEOSS, SINTEF Ocean, Digital, Norwegian University NTNU, IEEE France entwickelt.n (vlg. [32]). Zusammenfassend lässt sich sagen, dass bereits viele einzelne Projekte versuchen, Lösungen für zentrale Probleme in Bezug auf den Datenaustausch zwischen Unternehmen in der mariti- men Branche zu entwickeln. Die zentrale Herausforderung des Datenzugriffs und der Verfügbarkeit von relevanten Informa- tionen wurden somit erkannt. Allerdings zeigen die Vielzahl und Vielfalt der Projekte auch, dass ein einheitliches Vorgehen und zentrale Standards bislang weder verbreitet noch entwi- ckelt sind. 33 © Adobe Stock Befragung von maritimen Akteuren 4. updated_tasks.search_intent: Standards/Regeln für Onboard-Sensorik am fahrenden Zug, inkl. Zulassung, RAMS und funktionaler Sicherheit identifizieren.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Liefert normative Mindestanforderungen (RAMS/SIL/Zulassung) als zentrale Transfer-Voraussetzung.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Vergleich Wayside vs Onboard für typische Messfälle (Hotbox, Wheel Impact, Profil, Lager, Bremsen): Leistungskennwerte und Grenzen sammeln.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Ermöglicht Mapping der Messfunktionen und Performance-Anforderungen (Genauigkeit, Erfassungsrate, Fehlalarme) für Transferentscheidungen.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Technische Randbedingungen für Rolling-Stock-in-Motion-Sensorik: Vibration, Temperatur, EMV, Schutzart, Montage am Drehgestell/Radsatz.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Identifiziert Umwelt- und Integrationsbedingungen, die Wayside-Technologien beim Onboard-Einsatz limitieren oder Anpassungen erzwingen.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Energieversorgung, Datenlogger/Edge-AI und Kommunikation (TCMS, Ethernet, GSM-R/FRMCS) für Onboard-Monitoring im Betrieb recherchieren.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Klären der Systemarchitektur-Voraussetzungen (Strom, Rechenleistung, Schnittstellen, Datenübertragung) für skalierbaren Transfer.

updated_tasks.search_intent: Betriebs- und Instandhaltungsfaktoren: Kalibrierung, Datenqualität, Fehlalarme, Lebenszykluskosten und Prozessintegration (CBM/RCM) untersuchen.
updated_tasks.task_rationale: Ergänzt technische Anforderungen um organisatorische/ökonomische Faktoren, die Transfer in den Regelbetrieb ermöglichen.

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Type HBD HWD Temperature range Auto calibration, integrated Line detector Spectral range Meas. frequency Train speed Voltage Power consumption Sensor heater Ambient temp. 0°C to 150°C 80°C to 650°C Up to 150°C Up to 450°C 8 elements 3 – 5 µm 250 Hz Up to 500 km/h 24V DC ±2V Standby: 15W; Measurement: 45W Voltage: 48V AC; Power: 200W -30°C to +50°C The activation and deactivation of the infrared sensors is controlled by the rail contacts, mounted before and after the check-point measuring zone. the initiation of As mentioned above, the measuring process of all the microsystems is triggered by rail contacts. They represent simple strain-gauge force sensors, installed on the track before and after the check-point zone. Their signal is used by the road- side equipment for triggering the process for the on- track equipment. The axle counting is crucial for identification and linking the measured data to the corresponding vehicle and its axles. After the last axle in the train has passed the rail contact, installed after the check-point zone, the data measuring process is stopped and the acquired data is processed by the road-side equipment. Depending on the train length, the processing time may vary, but no more than couple of minutes. After the processing has been completed, the road- side equipment sends the results, along with alarms, remarks, etc. to the information users by the local network (fiber-optics) or Internet (wirelessly through GSM-R technology). In order to minimize the amount of data to be transferred, the system uses the XML Protocol. Depending on the type of information needed by the corresponding user, the data from transferred XML file is used to build up service databases, protocols, summaries, etc. The alarms about identified critical irregularities in a passing train are sent with priority to the next train station and the signaling systems in order the train to be stopped and checked before continuing. In case of power interruption, the system is backed up by alternative power supply (UPS) for couple of hours before complete shutdown. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: Power Mode: <1mA Normal Mode: 10mA In order to detect wheel defects, such as flat spots, out of roundness, polygonization, etc., accelerometer assemblies are used. The accelerometer assemblies (Fig. 7) are fixed to the side of the rail and detect peak amplitudes of the vertical acceleration, induced in the rail by the passing vehicles. The used accelerometers are typical 3-axis accelerometers and the vertical acceleration is measured in the Z-axis. The assemblies feature two power modes – Low Power Mode (standby) and Normal Mode (measuring). In Low Power Mode the power consumption is under 1mA, and in Normal Mode – 10mA. The switching between the two modes is also done by the rail contacts, which controls the power modes of the specialized force sensor. Fig. 7. Accelerometer assembly for detection of wheel defects. Fig. 6. Electronics of specialized strain-gauge force sensor. 3.3. Detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes The detection of hot boxes, wheels and brakes is achieved by using infrared technology [3]. The infrared sensors are commercial sensors manufactured by “voestalpine SIGNALING Siershahn” GmbH. In order to measure the temperature of the boxes, wheels and brakes, the microsystem relies on four infrared sensors, mounted on specialized sleeper on the track. Two for box temperature measuring (HBD) (Fig. 8) [3] and two for wheels and brakes temperature measuring (HWD). 4. INFRASTRUCTURE OF THE WAYSIDE MONITORING SYSTEM As Fig. 9 shows, a typical wayside monitoring system comprises of on-track equipment and road side equipment. The on-track equipment, depending on the client request, may include several measurement microsystems. All of them provide data to the road side equipment, which comprises a computer system for data acquisition and data processing. The measurement results are then transferred to the information user through local networks or Internet by using the XML Protocol. The information provided by the wayside monitoring system is used by the railway operators, haulers, railway signaling system, etc. in order to track and evaluate the condition of the passing vehicles. Fig. 8. Infrared sensor for detection of hot boxes. Specifications of the commercial infrared sensors are listed in Table 4 [3]. Table 4. Infrared sensors specifications. Plank-Wiedenbeck Erstellt am: 16.01.2011 | Stand des Wissens: 16.04.2025 Für moderne Verkehrstelematiksysteme ist zunehmend die Vernetzung und die damit einhergehende schnelle Datenübertragung von zentraler Bedeutung. Daten und die darin enthaltenen Informationen sollen möglichst ohne Zeitverzug vom Erfassungsort (Sensorik, fest installiert oder mobil) zur (Verkehrs-)Zentrale, von der Zentrale zum Verkehrsteilnehmenden oder von Verkehrsteilnehmenden zu Verkehrsteilnehmenden (beispielweise bei der Fahrzeug-zu-Fahrzeug- Kommunikation (Car-to-Car-Communication, C2C) übertragen werden. Systeme, die diese Informationen als Eingangsdaten nutzen, können so zeitnah Aussagen, beispielsweise über den aktuellen Zustand eines Verkehrssystems, liefern. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: efficiency and interference levels. Its steady fitness improvement and adaptability make it well-suited for scenarios where wide coverage is a priority alongside acceptable performance trade-offs. 1. Introduction High speed trains need to offer seamless connectivity and Quality of Service (QoS) to the passengers in order to have a satisfactory ex- perience during their journey. The necessity for optimal capacity and increased data rates is evident since there is a large number of users that are connected to the telecommunications network that is in operation. The growth in mobile data traffic is primarily driven by the global rise in mobile broadband subscriptions, alongside an increase in average data consumption per user. This surge is largely attributed to the streaming and sharing of high-definition videos and the growing adoption of immersive video content, which is often the case with passengers in high speed and long journey trains. A recent report pre- dicts that global mobile data traffic will quadruple between 2019 and 2025, reaching 160 exabytes per month, with an additional 53 exabytes per month expected from fixed wireless access traffic [1]. These pro- jections, combined with the diverse requirements of emerging servi- ces—such as low latency and high reliability. In particular, in rail communications 5G emerges [2] as a new communication paradigm dedicated to railways. From Global System for Mobile Communications-Railway (GSM-R) to Long Term Evolution- Railway (LTE-R) [3] rail communications are continuously advancing reaching 5G-R [4]. In such a way, bottlenecks of the communications in trains are addressed by introducing the advantages of 5G communica- tion to the passengers such as increased capacity, low latency, and higher throughput. Industry players such as mobile operators and equipment vendors are already aggressively trialing and deploying 5G-R technologies to meet evolving connectivity demands. Die drahtlose Übertragung bietet sich für mobile, sogenannte "Floating Car Data" (FCD), oder temporär installierte Sensorik sowie zur drahtlosen Anbindung von Sensoren an Streckenstationen an und lässt sich meist kostengünstiger installieren, da keine Leitungen verlegt oder gemietet werden müssen. Kappatos High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 155–162 networks that can scale for both expected and unforeseen scenarios. 5G- R is envisioned as a unified connectivity solution capable of handling growing data traffic and supporting diverse rail-based use cases. In general, 5G requires both advancements in core networks (e.g., network slicing, virtualization) and the development of the 5G New Radio (NR) air interface. This includes improvements in spectral efficiency, support for higher-order modulation, massive MIMO, and the use of millimeter wave frequencies. Additionally, 5G enhances network capacity through spectrum reuse and network densification, which involves increasing the number of cell sites, often small cells, to supplement existing macrocellular networks [5]. The deployment of 5G services requires the installation of new 5G infrastructure, known as next-generation Node-B (gNB), across the target area. Something similar is necessary for 5G-R installations. The process of selecting and configuring the locations where this 5G equipment will be installed is commonly referred to as 5G cellular planning [6]. This is a complex task that involves balancing factors such as costs, coverage area, and Electro-Magnetic Field (EMF) regulations. In high speed train scenarios, the installations should take place taking into consideration the speed and coverage while ensuring QoS. 5G-R which should be a standard at a European level as well, is a dedicated version of the 5G network that addresses the specific requirements of railway operations. It emphasizes ultra-reliable, low-latency commu- nication, which is essential for real-time train control, monitoring, and ensuring safety. Additionally, it supports applications like predictive maintenance, trackside surveillance, and passenger information sys- tems. While 5G-R is primarily optimized for railway use, its feature- s—such as high reliability, low latency, and large data capacity—can also cater to certain User Equipment (UE) needs, including real-time streaming, Internet of Things (IoT) connectivity, and remote control applications. However, these capabilities are generally designed with the operational demands of the railway industry in mind, rather
